game_meta.title: The Kailani Protocol: A Practitioner's Journey
game_meta.subtitle: A Core Humanitarian Standard Educational Game — 12-Month Practitioner Simulation
game_meta.description: An immersive, decision-based educational simulation spanning 12 months of humanitarian and development practice on Kailani Island. Players navigate emergency response, accountability challenges, coordination dilemmas, the humanitarian-development nexus, localization, and exit — across 12 main decisions, 3 conditional sub-decisions, and 82 scenes.
game_meta.target_audience: Humanitarian and development practitioners, team leaders, country directors, coordination staff
learning_objectives: Apply all nine CHS commitments across emergency, stabilisation and recovery phases
Navigate tension between institutional pressure and principled practice
Understand the humanitarian-development nexus and design trigger-based transition programming
Advance the Grand Bargain localization agenda through equitable partnership
Apply survivor-centred PSEA principles under difficult organisational conditions
Design responsible exit strategies that transfer power and assets to communities
Practise adaptive management based on real-time monitoring evidence
game_meta.predecessor: CHS_2024_game_v2.json — Kailani Rising (6 months, 9 decisions, 47 scenes)
game_meta.design_notes: Sub-decisions 2x, 5x and 10x appear conditionally based on flags set in earlier decisions. Sub-decision 2x appears if 'local_knowledge_built_in' is active (set by Decision 1 options B/C/D). Sub-decision 5x appears if 'survivor_centred_process' is active (set by Decision 5 options B/C/D). Sub-decision 10x appears if 'local_partnership_formalized' is active (set by Decision 10 options B/C/D). Total scenes: 77 (2 prologue + 12 decision + 3 sub-decision + 48 consequence + 3 bridge + 1 epilogue). design_notes value 82 in predecessor v2 referred to planned max including all flag variants.

player_character.name: Maya Chen
player_character.title: Country Director, HopeForward International — Kailani
player_character.background: Experienced humanitarian practitioner with 12 years in emergency response and recovery. Previously served as Programme Manager in South Asia, Horn of Africa and Pacific region. Known for principled leadership but frequently in tension with HQ's visibility and efficiency agenda. Speaks English, Mandarin and functional Kailani. Has a reputation for listening before deciding.
player_character.personality_note: Players embody Maya's perspective. Maya's decisions are the player's decisions.
institutional_pressures: HQ Regional Director (Philipe Mara) demands speed, visibility and donor compliance
HopeForward's institutional risk appetite is low — local partnerships are 'reputational risks'
Reporting deadlines and donor requirements create constant tension with participatory practice
Career implications: Maya's contract renewal depends on positive donor relations
assets: Deep respect for KCV's Filomena Tui and local knowledge
Established relationships with Amara Tofi and the NDMO
A field team of 12 (8 national, 4 international) committed to principled practice
Personal values strongly aligned with CHS — if she can hold them under pressure

setting.crisis: Cyclone Hina — Category 4, landfall 72 hours before the game begins

context.affected_population: 85,000 displaced at peak (Day 1); 40,000 remaining displaced by Month 3
context.geography: Coastal and inland zones; high-risk coastal areas cannot be rebuilt on
context.government_capacity: Functional but under-resourced NDMO; genuinely cooperative
context.local_civil_society: KCV (Kailani Civil Volunteers) — strong, trusted, first responder
context.market_functionality: 60% functional by Day 7; fully functional by Month 3
context.protection_risks: GBV near distribution points; disability exclusion; land tenure disputes

score_dimensions.label: Community Trust
score_dimensions.description: How much affected communities trust HopeForward's intentions, processes and people.
score_dimensions.low_score_consequence: Communities disengage, hide information, stop using complaint mechanisms.
score_dimensions.high_score_consequence: Communities co-design programming, surface problems early, hold HopeForward accountable.

score_dimensions.label: Programme Quality
score_dimensions.description: How appropriate, effective and adaptable the programme is to actual community needs.
score_dimensions.low_score_consequence: Programming misses real needs, creates dependency, wastes resources.
score_dimensions.high_score_consequence: Programming is adaptive, evidence-based, and leaves communities more resilient.

score_dimensions.label: Accountability
score_dimensions.description: How well HopeForward upholds its commitments to communities, partners and donors.
score_dimensions.low_score_consequence: Accountability failures undermine trust, attract formal complaints and donor sanction.
score_dimensions.high_score_consequence: Accountability culture enables open feedback, fast correction and systemic learning.

score_dimensions.label: Staff Wellbeing
score_dimensions.description: How well HopeForward maintains duty of care for its team, including national staff and partner organisations.
score_dimensions.low_score_consequence: Burnout, resignations, security incidents, and reduced programme quality.
score_dimensions.high_score_consequence: Motivated, stable team that maintains quality programming under pressure.

score_dimensions.label: Localisation & Partnership
score_dimensions.description: How well Maya transfers power, builds local capacity, and creates equitable partnerships with local actors.
score_dimensions.low_score_consequence: Dependency perpetuated, local actors marginalised, HopeForward remains the centre of everything.
score_dimensions.high_score_consequence: Local actors lead, own the programme, and continue the work after HopeForward leaves.

context_flags_system.description: Context flags are set by specific choices and shape subsequent decisions. Flags cannot be reversed once set. Some flags unlock conditional sub-decisions. Flags visible in situation summaries to reflect cumulative consequences of choices.

flags.set_by: Decision 1 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Communities expect continued meaningful participation. Complaint volumes increase as a positive accountability signal.
Communities understand their rights and HopeForward's responsibilities. Rights claims increase — a success indicator.

flags.set_by: Decision 1 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Programme is grounded in community knowledge. Unlocks Sub-decision 2x (Cash vs. In-Kind modality choice).
Women's leadership structures established in assessment. Women's savings cooperative identified and engaged.
Disability prevalence accurately captured (22% in Zone 3). Specific mobility and health needs documented.
Cash transfers implemented. Market system stimulus active. Beneficiary choice and dignity prioritised.
Local markets stimulated. Vendors expanding stock. Feeds into livelihood recovery and nexus programming.

flags.set_by: Decision 2 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Cross-cluster referral pathways functional. GBV and malnutrition cases connected to specialist services.

flags.set_by: Decision 2 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Monthly targeting reassessment in place. Mobile households and acute cases captured.
Community targeting and programme committees active. Become accountability infrastructure for Phase 2.
Protection analysis integrated into all programme activities. GBV referral pathways from targeting.

flags.set_by: Decision 3 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Conflict-sensitive siting completed. Land disputes identified and managed before shelter allocation.

flags.set_by: Decision 3 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: KCV and local builders as primary workforce. Employment income for 340 youth. Community investment in shelter outcomes.
Enhanced traditional building methods with cyclone/earthquake resilience. Communities can maintain and repair.
DRR training for 6,800 households. Community maintenance committees. Reduces vulnerability to future shocks.
Community DRR infrastructure and governance established. Foundation for sustainable exit.

flags.set_by: Decision 4 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: PSEA and protection policies briefed to all staff. Complaints mechanism tested and functional.

flags.set_by: Decision 4 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Beneficiary data handled ethically. Unencrypted storage issues identified and resolved.
Active do-no-harm review of all activities. Three risk points identified and addressed.
Community protection focal points trained. Sina Ulugia leads Zone 3 network. Most trusted safeguarding infrastructure on island.

flags.set_by: Decision 5 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Survivor-centred complaints process established. Unlocks Sub-decision 5x (PSEA Investigation Protocol).

flags.set_by: Decision 5 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Complaints mechanism tested, functional and trusted. Community usage increases as positive accountability signal.
Complainant-defined confidentiality protocols established. Sina Ulugia's preference for female investigator documented and honoured.
Formal PSEA investigation completed. Ratu Leva dismissed. Inter-agency learning case published.
Survivor-designed investigation — global best practice documentation. Sina's continued involvement in programme accountability.
KCV formally in coordination lead role. Northern coverage gap identified and closed.
KCV recognised as cluster co-lead. Institutional standing for KCV in all subsequent coordination and funding negotiations.
Formal localization commitment document. NDMO appreciation letter. Reference case for Decision 10 donor negotiations.

flags.set_by: Decision 7 options B, C or D
flags.effect_summary: Adaptive management system in place. Three programme crises identified and resolved through monitoring data.
Community monitors active in all zones. Community-led analysis produces findings external monitors cannot access.
Real-time monitoring dashboard. 72-hour early warning system for programme issues.

Culture survey completed. National staff in co-design. Values-aligned organisational culture documented.
Tane Fono on programme management team. National staff in leadership. Foundation for local-led Phase 3.
Community resource oversight. All financial irregularities disclosed and corrected. Donor trust at peak.
Community oversight committee established. All restrictive donor clauses renegotiated. Financial accountability as community power.

flags.effect_summary: Formal handover to KCV and/or NDMO. Assets transferred. Sustainability framework in place.

key_characters.name: Maya Chen
key_characters.role: Player Character — Country Director, HopeForward International
key_characters.description: Principled, experienced, navigating constant tension between institutional pressure and community accountability.

key_characters.name: Filomena Tui
key_characters.description: Local humanitarian leader with 15 years in community response. KCV was first responder before any INGO arrived. Deeply respected by communities, occasionally frustrated by INGO behaviour. Champion of localization.

key_characters.name: Amara Tofi
key_characters.description: Government counterpart. Functional and cooperative but under-resourced. Navigates political pressures from above. Wants genuine partnership with INGOs, not coordination theatre. Formally supports direct local funding.

key_characters.name: Philipe Mara
key_characters.description: Source of institutional pressure throughout the game. Prioritises donor visibility, speed and reputational protection. Not malicious — genuinely believes institutional sustainability requires compliance with donor preferences. Occasionally surprised when principled practice produces better donor outcomes.

key_characters.name: Dr. Sione Kepa
key_characters.role: Local Medical Officer and Health Cluster Coordinator
key_characters.description: Trusted local doctor. Primary source of clinical intelligence. Identifies acute malnutrition cases and disability needs invisible to external actors. Advocates for evidence-based programming.

key_characters.name: Hana Vili
key_characters.description: Leader of the Zone 3 community. First person to challenge HopeForward's exclusion of her community. Becomes the community's primary voice for accountability throughout the response. Elected co-chair of the Assessment Reference Group (Decision 1D).

key_characters.name: Dr. Rangi Hau
key_characters.description: Academic researcher specialising in participatory monitoring and community-led evaluation. Arrives as MEAL advisor by Month 2. Designer of the real-time community monitoring system (Decision 7). Publishes the monitoring methodology — downloaded 400 times in the first week.

key_characters.name: Elena Vasquez
key_characters.role: UN Protection Cluster Lead and PSEA Focal Point
key_characters.description: Experienced protection specialist. Formally advocates for survivor-centred PSEA approaches. Contacts Maya after GBV incidents at distribution points (Decision 4). Manages the inter-agency PSEA mechanism in Decisions 5 and 5x.

key_characters.name: Tane Fono
key_characters.role: KCV Youth Coordinator — Emerging Local Leader
key_characters.description: 23-year-old KCV youth leader. Leads the youth construction team in Decision 3. Becomes community feedback facilitator. Joins HopeForward programme management team (Decision 8D). Represents next-generation humanitarian leadership. Resigned when partnership equity failed (Decision 10A) — or led the exit event (Decision 12D).

key_characters.name: Sina Ulugia
key_characters.description: 23-year-old woman from Zone 3. Filed the formal PSEA complaint against Ratu Leva (Decision 5). Became the community's Zone 3 protection focal point (Decision 4D). Her case drives sub-decision 5x. Her experience shapes how HopeForward redesigns its complaints mechanism. Continues as community accountability monitor in Phase 3.

key_characters.name: James Okafor
key_characters.description: Operationally focused. Prioritises efficiency and procurement compliance. Discovers the $47k grant misuse (Decision 9). Not malicious — represents the operational pressure that competes with principled practice.

key_characters.name: Maria Santos
key_characters.role: UNICEF Programme Officer and Cluster Coordinator
key_characters.description: Represents the UN coordination architecture. Initially proposes a UN-centric coordination framework (Decision 6). Eventually accepts KCV co-lead after formal advocacy. Institutionally cautious but responsive to principled argument.

chs_reference_summary.note: Full CHS text should be consulted alongside this game. This summary provides orientation only.

commitments.title: Participation
commitments.core_idea: Affected communities meaningfully participate in all decisions about their response.

commitments.title: Appropriate Assistance
commitments.core_idea: Assistance meets actual needs and adapts as needs change.

commitments.title: Strengthening Local Capacities
commitments.core_idea: Response builds — not replaces — local systems and capacities.

commitments.title: Do No Harm
commitments.core_idea: Work is based on understanding of context and causes no harm.

commitments.title: Complaints Mechanisms
commitments.core_idea: Safe, accessible complaints mechanisms that are responsive and learning-oriented.

commitments.title: Coordination
commitments.core_idea: Collaborative, complementary work with local and national actors as primary responders.

commitments.title: Learning & Accountability
commitments.core_idea: Evidence-based, adaptive programming with shared learning.

commitments.title: Staff Wellbeing
commitments.core_idea: Staff are supported, fairly treated and the culture reflects organisational values.

commitments.title: Resource Management
commitments.core_idea: Resources are managed transparently, efficiently and for their intended purpose.

scenes.title: Day One — Cyclone Hina's Aftermath
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~200 words. Maya Chen's plane descends through post-cyclone cloud over Kailani. Cyclone Hina struck 72 hours ago. 85,000 displaced. Coastal villages destroyed. Inland flooding has cut the main road. The National Disaster Management Office is overwhelmed. HopeForward has been invited to lead the coordinated INGO response. Maya has 12 months of funding, a four-person team, a mandate to respond to all nine CHS commitments — and a phone full of messages from an impatient Regional Director.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Day One — Cyclone Hina's Aftermath: transition scene]
characters_introduced: Maya Chen — HopeForward Country Director, protagonist
Filomena Tui — KCV Director, local humanitarian leader (first call)
Philipe Mara — HopeForward Regional Director, HQ (first voicemail)

scenes.title: The Kailani Context — Who Is Already Here
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Briefing at Port Hana emergency hub. Kailani Civil Volunteers (KCV) arrived Hour 2, before any INGO. They conducted search-and-rescue and distributed water using their own network. Amara Tofi (NDMO Director) needs international support but is wary of agencies that undermine national authority. Philipe Mara (HQ Regional Director) wants a Day 1 press release. Three INGOs are competing for cluster lead status. Filomena Tui is at the door, waiting to brief Maya on what communities actually need. Maya must decide: who does she listen to first?
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Kailani Context — Who Is Already Here: transition scene]

scenes.title: Decision 1 — Community Assessment & Inclusive Participation

chs_commitment.title: People and communities can exercise their rights and participate in actions and decisions that affect them.

requirements_in_play.1.1: Ensure DEI considerations are integrated, with attention to the most marginalised.
requirements_in_play.1.2: Regularly share information including rights information.
requirements_in_play.1.3: Communicate in accessible languages and formats.
requirements_in_play.1.4: Ensure participation is meaningful and matches community preferences.
requirements_in_play.1.6: Establish a coherent organisational participation approach.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Three displacement zones: Zone 1 (22,000 coastal fishing families, Kailani-language primary), Zone 2 (18,000 inland agricultural households, Tevita dialect), Zone 3 (5,500 people including 1,100 with disabilities, 800 elderly, 2,200 female-headed households — housed in the government sports complex). KCV's Filomena knows Zone 3 intimately. Elder Hana Vili is asking why HopeForward hasn't visited Zone 3. Amara Tofi wants a consolidated INGO-government assessment within 48 hours. Philipe Mara: 'Show donors we're responding, Maya.' Filomena offers a participatory community dialogue — 4 days, all zones, all languages, disability-inclusive. Dr. Sione Kepa warns Zone 3 health needs are invisible in any secondary dataset.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional speed (48-hour rapid assessment) vs. meaningful participation (4-day inclusive process reaching all communities).
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 1 — Community Assessment & Inclusive Participation: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Rapid government-led survey (48 hours)
options.description_summary: Accept Amara's method: English written questionnaire, government enumerators, Zones 1–2 only, 48-hour turnaround.
options.chs_learning_note: The fastest assessment reliably excludes the most marginalised. Clean numbers are not the same as accurate numbers.

options.label: KCV-led community dialogues with women and youth focus
options.description_summary: Partner with KCV: facilitated dialogues in all three zones. Women's groups and youth prioritised. Multi-language. 4 days. Women's leadership structure established for future programming.
options.chs_learning_note: KCV's existing trust with Zone 3 is a programme asset. Investing in participatory assessment prevents expensive re-targeting.

options.label: Disability-inclusive mixed-methods assessment with rights briefings
options.description_summary: Focus groups, KII, observation with disability/elderly inclusion protocol. Rights briefings in Kailani, Tevita and sign language. 5 days.
options.chs_learning_note: Purpose-built inclusive methods find what standard surveys miss. Disability prevalence is routinely under-counted by 50–200% in rapid assessments.

options.label: Full participatory assessment: rights-first, community-governed, all zones
options.description_summary: Co-design with KCV, community leaders, women's groups and disability network. Establish Assessment Reference Group. Rights briefings as first step. Household surveys + focus groups + participatory mapping across all zones in all languages. 6 days.
options.chs_learning_note: CHS 1.6 calls for a coherent organisational approach — not a one-off consultation. The Reference Group becomes permanent programme governance infrastructure.

scenes.title: Invisible in the Data
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. The government survey returns 40,200 responses in 48 hours. Clean, reportable, donor-ready. Zone 3 is a footnote: 'Assessed separately.' But it wasn't. Hana Vili stops Maya outside the cluster meeting: 'We were never asked.' Distribution plans are already printed. 5,500 people are not on any list.
scenes.consequence_summary: Zone 3 entirely absent from programme design. Distribution plan covers Zones 1–2 only.
scenes.delayed_impact: Missed households create grievances by Week 2. KCV trust strained. Corrective re-targeting required at additional cost.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Rapid assessments that skip marginalised groups produce data gaps that become programme gaps.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Ensure diversity, equity and inclusion considerations are integrated, with attention to the most marginalised.' — CHS 1.1
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Invisible in the Data: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: The Women Speak First
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. KCV-led dialogues surface critical data: women in Zone 2 avoid communal water points due to GBV risk. Zone 3 has 22% disability prevalence — twice the national average. A women's savings cooperative — unknown to any external actor — is already managing community food distribution. The assessment takes 4 days but produces the most accurate needs profile on the island. Philipe Mara's day-two call goes unanswered. The data is worth the wait.
scenes.consequence_summary: High-quality data across all three zones. Women's safety concerns and cooperative identified. KCV trust deepened.
scenes.delayed_impact: Women's savings cooperative becomes implementation partner in Phase 2. Local knowledge base shapes all future decisions.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Quality participatory assessment surfaces community capacities that become programme assets.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Ensure people's participation in decisions and actions is meaningful for them.' — CHS 1.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Women Speak First: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: No One Left in the Footnotes
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Mixed-methods assessment takes 5 days and produces a dataset that surprises the cluster: disability prevalence 22% in Zone 3, acute malnutrition in 4% of children under 5, three GBV cases identified by KII. Rights briefings in Kailani and Tevita spark visible relief — many displaced people did not know they could make complaints. Amara Tofi asks for a copy of the rights briefing to use in NDMO communications.
scenes.consequence_summary: Comprehensive inclusive data. Rights information broadly shared. Disability, malnutrition and GBV cases identified.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community awareness of rights increases complaint volume in Phase 2 — a sign of accountability success, not failure.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Purpose-built inclusive methods find what standard surveys miss. Rights briefings are part of the assessment, not an add-on.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Communicate in languages and formats that are accessible, understandable and respectful.' — CHS 1.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [No One Left in the Footnotes: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: The Assessment Reference Group Meets
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Assessment Reference Group convenes on Day 3. Hana Vili elected co-chair. Disability representative and two youth members join. Six days of assessment produce the most comprehensive community profile of any agency on the island. Crucially, the Reference Group will continue meeting throughout the entire 12-month response. Filomena tells Maya: 'This is the most serious commitment to accountability I have ever seen from an INGO.' HQ's Philipe Mara is unhappy about the 6-day delay — until he sees the data quality.
scenes.consequence_summary: Full participatory assessment with permanent governance structure. All zones, all languages, all demographics.
scenes.delayed_impact: Assessment Reference Group becomes programme governance body for all 12 months, shaping every subsequent decision.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Participation governance infrastructure created at assessment stage lasts through all 12 months.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure transparent information-sharing and meaningful participation.' — CHS 1.6
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Assessment Reference Group Meets: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Sub-Decision 2x — Cash vs. In-Kind Programming Modality

chs_commitment.title: Communities receive assistance appropriate to their needs; local capacities are strengthened.

requirements_in_play.2.1: Needs are assessed in a participatory and impartial way.
requirements_in_play.2.2: Assistance is designed to address assessed needs.
requirements_in_play.2.5: Response builds on local coping strategies.
requirements_in_play.3.2: Local actors are involved as equal partners.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. The high-quality assessment data enables an informed modality choice. Market assessment (commissioned alongside community assessment) shows markets 60% functional. Vendors have stock; purchasing power is destroyed. Protection concern: three GBV incidents reported near the main market in Week 1. Women in Zone 2 avoiding the market. Filomena: 'Cash means dignity and choice.' Dr. Sione: 'In-kind guarantees nutrition adequacy — markets can fail.' Hana Vili: 'Ask the women. They will tell you.' Market monitoring team (Dr. Rangi Hau's university team) ready to provide real-time price data.
scenes.tension_statement: Programme efficiency (in-kind logistics) vs. beneficiary dignity (cash/choice) vs. protection risks for women vs. nutritional assurance.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Sub-Decision 2x — Cash vs. In-Kind Programming Modality: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: In-kind food and NFI distribution
options.description_summary: Standard emergency food baskets and NFI kits. Nutrition-grade, pre-verified. Avoids market-based protection risks. Removes beneficiary choice.
options.chs_learning_note: In-kind is appropriate when markets are non-functional. At 60% functionality, blanket in-kind forfeits dignity and local economic stimulation.

options.label: Cash transfers with market monitoring and safe distribution points
options.description_summary: Unrestricted cash via female-led distribution team. Safe cash-out at community centres (not open market). Concurrent market price monitoring. KCV addresses market safety for women.
options.chs_learning_note: Cash with protection mainstreaming respects dignity, supports local economies, and gives communities control over their own recovery.

options.label: Hybrid: cash for most, in-kind for disability/elderly/acute nutrition
options.description_summary: Universal cash plus targeted in-kind for people with disabilities, elderly and acute malnutrition cases identified in assessment. Dual system, maximum equity.
requirements_missed: 3.2 — dual system adds KCV management burden
options.chs_learning_note: Hybrid approaches recognise that no single modality works for everyone. Quality assessment makes this differentiation feasible.

scenes.title: Boxes Delivered, Agency Removed
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Distribution runs smoothly. Donor photos are good. But at the community feedback session, a Zone 2 woman stands up: 'The box food — my family does not eat half of it. My neighbour's baby needs different formula. Can we choose?' Markets are functional three blocks away. HopeForward chose logistics convenience over beneficiary dignity.
scenes.consequence_summary: Physical needs met. Low satisfaction in feedback. Markets not stimulated.
scenes.delayed_impact: Market vendor exodus by Month 3. In-kind programme crowds out local traders who needed the economic stimulus.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Standard in-kind at 60% market functionality forfeits dignity and local economic recovery for operational convenience.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Assistance is designed to address the assessed needs of affected communities.' — CHS 2.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Boxes Delivered, Agency Removed: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Cash, Community, and Control
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Safe cash-out points in community centres attract women who had avoided the open market. Market vendors report 40% sales increase within two weeks. KCV's female distribution team runs the safest, most trusted distribution point on the island. Three market vendors expand stock anticipating continued demand. Filomena: 'In 15 years, this is the first response that asked women and then changed its programme.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Cash with safe access. Market vendors stimulated. Women report safety and high satisfaction.
scenes.delayed_impact: Market system strength feeds into Phase 3 livelihood programming and Decision 11 nexus transition.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Cash with protection mainstreaming simultaneously meets immediate needs, supports market recovery, and strengthens women's economic agency.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Programmes build on and do not undermine local coping strategies and capacities.' — CHS 2.5
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Cash, Community, and Control: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Differentiated Response, Differentiated Dignity
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. The dual system strains KCV logistics but the community response is clear: people feel seen. Elderly individuals receive doorstep in-kind delivery. Everyone else exercises choice. Zero acute malnutrition cases in the first monitoring round — a rare outcome. Amara Tofi asks for a methodology briefing to inform NDMO national guidelines. Dr. Rangi Hau publishes a note on the model for the regional humanitarian network.
scenes.consequence_summary: Differentiated needs met. Zero acute malnutrition. Government interest in methodology.
scenes.delayed_impact: NDMO interest in methodology creates government partnership for Phase 2 nexus planning.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Hybrid modalities require management investment but achieve equity outcomes that uniform approaches cannot.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Assistance is designed to address the assessed needs, including the most marginalised.' — CHS 2.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Differentiated Response, Differentiated Dignity: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Decision 2 — Distribution Targeting & Adaptive Programming

chs_commitment.title: Communities receive assistance that is appropriate, relevant and effective for their needs.

requirements_in_play.2.1: Needs are assessed periodically, impartially and participatorily.
requirements_in_play.2.2: Assistance is adjusted to changing needs of individuals and households.
requirements_in_play.2.3: Assistance considers the needs of all, including most marginalised.
requirements_in_play.2.5: Response builds on local coping strategies.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. First distribution complete. Week 2 monitoring reveals: 3,400 highly mobile fisher households were missed in initial targeting. 17 households with acute malnutrition are not on the list. Two communities have recovered faster than expected — continued distribution risks dependency. A women's cooperative in Zone 2 is selling surplus NFI for cash — a sign of over-supply. Dr. Sione identifies a cluster of malnourished children in Zone 3 who slipped through. Amara wants all agencies to use a single consolidated government list — it is already outdated.
scenes.tension_statement: Administrative consistency (government list) vs. evidence-based adaptive targeting following actual need.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 2 — Distribution Targeting & Adaptive Programming: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Maintain original government targeting list unchanged
options.description_summary: Use Amara's list as-is. No re-assessment. Administratively simple, inter-agency consistent, but perpetuates known gaps and over-supplies recovering communities.
options.chs_learning_note: Targeting fidelity to an outdated list is a political choice disguised as operational necessity.

options.label: Adaptive targeting: monthly reassessment with supplemental list
options.description_summary: Monthly reassessment using KCV monitors. Remove graduating households (with transition plan). Add mobile fishers and malnutrition cases. Negotiate with Amara to use government list as base with HopeForward supplemental targeting.
requirements_missed: 2.5 — dependency risk in recovering communities not fully addressed
options.chs_learning_note: Adaptive targeting catches evolving need and prevents resources flowing to the wrong people for the wrong duration.

options.label: Community-verified targeting with transparency mechanism
options.description_summary: Co-design targeting criteria with community committees. Publish criteria publicly. Allow community challenge process. Remove recovering communities after 30-day transition. Mobile registration for fisher households.
options.chs_learning_note: Community verification of targeting is both participatory and anti-corruption — communities know who their most vulnerable neighbours are.

options.label: Nexus-aware graduated targeting with protection referral system
options.description_summary: Tiered targeting: emergency assistance for acute needs; livelihood support for recovering households transitioning off list; protection referrals for GBV and malnutrition. Exit criteria agreed with communities and NDMO.
options.chs_learning_note: Best practice targeting anticipates different recovery trajectories and designs programming to match from Week 1.

scenes.title: The Locked List
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Government list unchanged. By Week 3, Dr. Sione has identified 17 malnourished children off-list. Three fisher communities submit complaints to KCV: they were never registered. The Zone 2 women's cooperative quietly redistributes surplus NFI — a community correction to HopeForward's administrative failure. Amara is satisfied. Donors have clean numbers. Communities have growing grievances.
scenes.consequence_summary: Original list maintained. 17 malnourished children missed. Fisher communities unregistered.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community trust erodes by Month 2. Three formal complaints received. Corrective action required at additional cost.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Targeting fidelity to outdated lists is a systemic failure masked by operational convenience.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Assistance is adjusted to changing needs of individuals, households and communities.' — CHS 2.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Locked List: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Monthly Reassessment Saves Lives
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. First monthly targeting review adds 3,400 fisher households, removes 800 recovering families with transition plans. Dr. Sione's 17 malnutrition cases registered by Day 12. Amara initially resistant but agrees when she sees the data. The Nutrition Cluster coordinator calls HopeForward's targeting 'the gold standard this response needed.'
scenes.consequence_summary: 3,400 mobile households added. 17 malnutrition cases registered. 800 recovering households transitioned.
scenes.delayed_impact: HopeForward targeting methodology becomes reference model. NDMO requests cross-agency adoption.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Adaptive targeting with regular reassessment catches evolving need and prevents both gaps and dependency.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Assistance is periodically reassessed to adjust for changes in context and need.' — CHS 2.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Monthly Reassessment Saves Lives: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Communities Know Who Needs Help
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Community targeting committees form across all three zones within 5 days. Three households voluntarily exit after self-verification. Two previously unregistered households added by community request. A family hiding a disability due to stigma is now included in protection referrals — surfaced by the community process, not external assessment. Filomena: 'The community did in two days what took HopeForward three weeks to attempt administratively.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Community committees active in all zones. Self-verification reduces targeting errors. Hidden disability case surfaced.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community committees become accountability infrastructure for Phase 2 complaints mechanisms and PSEA.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Community verification achieves accuracy and accountability outcomes that external verification cannot replicate.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Assistance and protection activities consider the needs of all people, including the most marginalised.' — CHS 2.3

scenes.title: Graduated Targeting as Resilience Bridge
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Tiered targeting system is complex to explain but powerful in effect. 17 households on emergency track receive acute support. 42 recovering households linked to livelihood starter packages. Three GBV cases connected to Elena Vasquez's protection cluster. Dr. Rangi Hau: 'The most sophisticated targeting framework I have seen outside academic literature — implemented in the field at Week 2.' Maya briefs the entire cluster on the methodology.
scenes.consequence_summary: Multi-tiered system: emergency, transition and recovery tracks. GBV cases referred. Cluster briefed.
scenes.delayed_impact: Nexus framework from Week 2 creates conditions for smooth transition programming in Decision 11 (Month 6).
scenes.learning_point_summary: Nexus-aware targeting from Week 1 anticipates the transition from emergency to recovery programming.
scenes.chs_quote: 'The response builds on and does not undermine local coping strategies and capacities.' — CHS 2.5

scenes.title: Decision 3 — Shelter, Resilience & Local Ownership

chs_commitment.title: Communities and people are not negatively affected and are more prepared, resilient and less at risk as a result of the work.

requirements_in_play.3.1: Work does not exacerbate existing tensions or vulnerabilities.
requirements_in_play.3.2: Local capacities and coping mechanisms are built on.
requirements_in_play.3.3: Activities prepare communities for similar crises.
requirements_in_play.3.4: Local actors are central partners in design and delivery.
requirements_in_play.3.5: Activities build local capacity to respond to future needs.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Shelter assessment complete: 8,200 homes partially or fully destroyed. Two options on the table from the CCCM cluster: (a) standardised imported shelter kits — fast, donor-approved, but built by external contractors; (b) enhanced traditional building methods using local materials and KCV's construction training programme. Filomena's team includes 12 certified local builders who trained in resilient construction after the 2018 cyclone. Tane Fono (KCV Youth Coordinator) has identified 340 young men and women ready to work as construction assistants. Amara raises a conflict-sensitivity concern: two adjacent communities have a land boundary dispute — if shelter is allocated along current settlement lines, it could entrench a conflict that predates the cyclone.
scenes.tension_statement: Speed and standardisation (imported kits, external contractors) vs. local ownership and resilience (KCV-led, traditional methods, conflict-sensitive).
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 3 — Shelter, Resilience & Local Ownership: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Standardised imported shelter kits via external contractors
options.description_summary: Procure UNHCR-standard imported kits. External construction company. Fast delivery, donor-compliant, internationally verifiable quality. No local economic benefit.
options.chs_learning_note: Speed and standardisation often serve donor reporting more than community recovery. Imported kits that sit unused because they don't fit local housing patterns are a well-documented failure.

options.label: Hybrid: imported kits with KCV construction team as primary workforce
options.description_summary: Use approved shelter kits but contract KCV and local builders for construction. Employment income for 340 youth. External technical oversight. Conflict-sensitivity assessment commissioned.
requirements_missed: 3.1 — land conflict analysis started but not yet integrated
3.5 — some but not full capacity transfer
options.chs_learning_note: Including local builders as primary workforce creates economic multipliers and dignity alongside physical shelter outcomes.

options.label: KCV-led construction using enhanced traditional methods with conflict-sensitive siting
options.description_summary: Commission KCV to lead shelter using enhanced traditional materials and earthquake/cyclone-resistant techniques. Conflict-sensitive siting analysis completed before allocation. Tane Fono's youth team as trained workforce.
options.chs_learning_note: Traditional building knowledge, when enhanced with resilience standards, produces shelters that communities know how to maintain, repair and improve.

options.label: Community-owned resilient construction programme with DRR integration
options.description_summary: Co-design with communities, KCV and local government: enhanced traditional materials, conflict-sensitive allocation, full DRR and climate resilience training for all households, youth employment programme, community maintenance committees established.
options.chs_learning_note: CHS 3 is about leaving communities stronger — not just with a roof, but with the skills, knowledge and structures to weather future shocks.

scenes.title: Kits That Don't Fit
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Imported kits arrive in 10 days. But 38% of households reject them — the design doesn't fit Kailani sleeping arrangements. A land conflict erupts when two households are allocated adjacent plots that straddle the disputed boundary. Amara calls Maya: 'I told you about the land issue.' External contractors leave after 3 weeks. No local capacity built. No community maintenance plan.
scenes.consequence_summary: 38% kit rejection. Land conflict erupts. External contractors complete work and leave. No capacity transfer.
scenes.delayed_impact: Land dispute escalates by Month 2. Requires separate mediation process and delayed shelter completion.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Standardised imported kits optimise logistics while ignoring cultural fit, local capacity and conflict dynamics.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Work does not exacerbate existing tensions or vulnerabilities.' — CHS 3.1
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Kits That Don't Fit: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Local Hands, Imported Design
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. KCV construction team of 340 youth provides employment and builds community investment in shelter outcomes. Quality is good. Conflict-sensitivity assessment is commissioned — it arrives after allocation. One land dispute surfaces but is resolved with community mediation facilitated by Hana Vili. Filomena notes the youth employment programme has become the most popular thing HopeForward has done.
scenes.consequence_summary: KCV youth employed. One land dispute resolved. Kits delivered with community involvement.
scenes.delayed_impact: Youth employment programme creates local goodwill that buffers tension in Phase 2.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Local builders as primary workforce creates economic multipliers and community ownership even when kit design is standardised.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Local capacities and coping mechanisms are built on as the starting point of a response.' — CHS 3.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Local Hands, Imported Design: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Roots and Resistance
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Conflict-sensitive siting analysis reshapes the allocation plan — 120 households are relocated to plots that respect traditional land boundaries. Shelter built using enhanced traditional methods takes slightly longer but communities know every joint, beam and technique. Tane Fono's youth team becomes a skilled construction unit. Amara asks KCV to present the methodology at the NDMO national shelter workshop.
scenes.consequence_summary: Conflict-sensitive siting. Traditional materials with resilience upgrade. Youth trained and employed.
scenes.delayed_impact: Skilled youth construction unit becomes available for Phase 3 housing reconstruction in Decision 11.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Traditional building knowledge enhanced with resilience standards produces culturally appropriate shelter communities can maintain.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Activities help communities prepare for similar crises in the future.' — CHS 3.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Roots and Resistance: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Communities That Can Weather the Next Storm
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Community maintenance committees form in all three zones by Week 4. DRR training reaches 6,800 households. Tane Fono's youth unit completes the construction and immediately begins a community DRR facilitator training course. The shelter programme has produced not just buildings but knowledge, skills and institutional structures. Amara tells Maya: 'When the next cyclone comes — and it will — this community will not need us the way they needed us this time.'
scenes.consequence_summary: DRR training for 6,800 households. Community maintenance committees. Youth construction unit qualified.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community DRR infrastructure dramatically reduces vulnerability in Decision 12 exit assessment.
scenes.learning_point_summary: CHS 3 asks organisations to leave communities stronger. This option defines what 'stronger' means in practice.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Organisations' activities help communities prepare for and cope with similar crises in future.' — CHS 3.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Communities That Can Weather the Next Storm: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Decision 4 — Protection, Do No Harm & PSEA Foundations

chs_commitment.title: Communities and people are protected from harm, exploitation and abuse.

requirements_in_play.4.1: Staff have the knowledge and skills to identify and respond to harm and abuse.
requirements_in_play.4.2: Systems prevent and detect harm to the people organisations work with.
requirements_in_play.4.3: Risks from work are known and minimised.
requirements_in_play.4.4: PSEA and child safeguarding policies are current, promoted and enforced.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Three weeks in. Elena Vasquez (UN Protection Cluster Lead) contacts Maya. Five GBV incidents reported near distribution points. Two child protection concerns logged with UNICEF. One HopeForward community liaison officer has been observed accepting 'facilitation' from a community leader in exchange for targeting inclusion — a potential PSEA red flag, reported by Filomena. HopeForward's PSEA policy exists on paper but has not been briefed to field staff. Complaints mechanism is a phone number on a poster — nobody has tested whether it works. Elena wants HopeForward to sign the inter-agency PSEA commitment. James Okafor (HQ logistics) says a full PSEA rollout will take 3 weeks and delay phase 2 activities.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional convenience (delay PSEA, keep pace) vs. principled protection (act on red flags now, build safeguards).
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 4 — Protection, Do No Harm & PSEA Foundations: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Note concerns, maintain pace, address PSEA in Phase 2 planning
options.description_summary: Acknowledge Elena's concerns. Continue current pace. Schedule full PSEA rollout for Month 2. Investigate the liaison officer concern 'informally'.
options.chs_learning_note: Deferring PSEA activation after specific red flags are identified is not a scheduling decision — it is an accountability failure.

options.label: Immediate PSEA staff briefing, complaints mechanism test, sign inter-agency commitment
options.description_summary: Three-day PSEA briefing for all staff. Test and activate complaints mechanism. Sign Elena's inter-agency commitment. Investigate liaison officer concern formally.
options.chs_learning_note: PSEA activation after red flags requires urgency, not scheduling. The complaints mechanism must function before communities need to use it.

options.label: Protection mainstreaming across all activities with formal do-no-harm review
options.description_summary: Suspend liaison officer pending investigation. Full PSEA staff briefing. Do-no-harm review of all current activities. Protection risk register established. GBV referral pathway activated with Elena's cluster.
options.chs_learning_note: Do-no-harm requires active analysis of risks — not passive assurance that risks are unlikely.

options.label: Transformative protection: survivor-led safeguarding, community complaint ownership
options.description_summary: Suspend and investigate liaison officer. PSEA and safeguarding briefing for all staff and KCV partners. Protection risk register. Community-owned complaint focal points trained in each zone (including by Sina Ulugia's survivor network in Zone 3). GBV referral pathway with Elena. Child protection protocol with UNICEF.
options.chs_learning_note: Survivor-informed safeguarding design outperforms institutional safeguarding systems because communities know their own protection landscape.

scenes.title: The Red Flag Left to Fly
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Three weeks pass. The liaison officer continues his work. By Month 2, Sina Ulugia — a 23-year-old survivor in Zone 3 — submits a formal complaint alleging the officer solicited favours in exchange for targeting inclusion. Two other women submit similar complaints within 72 hours. Elena Vasquez contacts Maya: 'I told you in Week 3.' HopeForward faces a safeguarding crisis it could have interrupted if it had acted when the first red flag appeared.
scenes.consequence_summary: PSEA not activated. Liaison officer continues. By Month 2, three formal complaints including Sina Ulugia's.
scenes.delayed_impact: Sina Ulugia's complaint becomes the central accountability challenge of Decision 5 and Sub-decision 5x.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Deferring PSEA activation after identified red flags is not a scheduling matter — it is an accountability failure with real survivor impact.
scenes.chs_quote: 'PSEA and child safeguarding policies are current, promoted and enforced throughout the organisation.' — CHS 4.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Red Flag Left to Fly: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Systems Before the Crisis
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. PSEA briefing completes in three days. Complaints mechanism tested: the first call reveals the phone number on the poster connects to an unanswered voicemail. It is fixed. Liaison officer investigation opened formally — he is suspended pending review. Elena signs HopeForward onto the inter-agency PSEA commitment. James Okafor is frustrated by the delay to Phase 2 logistics — until he hears about the liaision officer finding.
scenes.consequence_summary: PSEA briefing complete. Complaints mechanism activated and tested. Liaison officer suspended.
scenes.delayed_impact: Functional complaints system handles Sina Ulugia's case in Decision 5 with existing infrastructure.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Functional complaints systems must be tested, not assumed. Activation after red flags is the minimum responsible response.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Systems prevent and detect harm, abuse and exploitation of the people organisations work with.' — CHS 4.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Systems Before the Crisis: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Do No Harm Means Actually Looking
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. The do-no-harm review of current activities surfaces three risk points: Zone 3 distribution creates crowds near an area with reported GBV. The beneficiary database contains personal data stored on an unencrypted spreadsheet on a shared drive. A targeting criterion inadvertently disadvantages female-headed households. All three are addressed. The protection risk register becomes a live document updated weekly by the field team. Elena Vasquez calls it 'the most serious do-no-harm practice I have seen in an INGO in this region.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Three risk points identified and addressed. Protection risk register live. GBV referral pathway with Elena.
scenes.delayed_impact: Protection risk register informs PSEA approach in Decision 5 and MEAL methodology in Decision 7.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Do-no-harm requires active risk analysis of current activities — not passive assurance that harm is unlikely.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Risks from the work of the organisation that could harm people and communities are known and minimised.' — CHS 4.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Do No Harm Means Actually Looking: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Protection Owned by Communities
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Community protection focal points trained in all three zones. Sina Ulugia — a 23-year-old survivor who had been quietly documenting concerns for weeks — becomes the Zone 3 focal point. She had already identified the liaison officer's behaviour and was waiting for a safe channel to report. Her network surfaces two more cases within 48 hours. Elena Vasquez: 'You just built the most trusted safeguarding infrastructure on this island, and it cost you three days and some flipchart paper.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Community protection focal points in all zones. Sina Ulugia leads Zone 3 network. Survivor-designed system.
scenes.delayed_impact: Sina Ulugia's network becomes the most trusted accountability infrastructure in Phase 2, shaping Decision 5.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Survivor-informed safeguarding design outperforms institutional systems because communities know their own protection landscape.
scenes.chs_quote: 'People have access to safe and responsive mechanisms to report concerns and complaints.' — CHS 4.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Protection Owned by Communities: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Month 2 — Stabilisation Begins
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. One month in. The emergency phase has stabilised. 58,000 people remain displaced. 27,000 have self-returned or found alternative shelter. HopeForward has a functioning programme — but the real test of accountability is about to begin. A formal complaint has been submitted via the complaints mechanism (or is about to be, depending on earlier choices). Coordination structures are consolidating. Donor reporting deadlines are approaching. Maya's team of 12 (8 national, 4 international) is showing signs of stress. The next four decisions will define HopeForward's accountability, coordination and learning practice.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Month 2 — Stabilisation Begins: transition scene]

scenes.title: Decision 5 — Survivor-Centered Complaints Mechanism & PSEA

chs_commitment.title: Communities and people have access to safe and responsive mechanisms to handle complaints.

requirements_in_play.5.1: People know about and trust the complaints mechanism.
requirements_in_play.5.2: Complaints are handled in a timely, fair and confidential manner.
requirements_in_play.5.3: Reports of PSEA are handled in line with inter-agency commitments.
requirements_in_play.5.4: Complainants are not at risk of retaliation or harm.
requirements_in_play.5.5: Accountability and learning from complaints drives programme improvement.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Sina Ulugia (23, female, Zone 3) has submitted a formal written complaint. A HopeForward field officer — Ratu Leva, well-connected, 8 years in the community, well-liked by national staff — allegedly solicited personal favours from women in exchange for targeting inclusion. Two other women have made verbal statements to KCV. Elena Vasquez (PSEA focal point) has contacted Maya. Ratu Leva denies the allegations and has already spoken to three community leaders who are vouching for him. James Okafor worries about reputational risk if the case becomes public before it is 'resolved'. Philipe Mara from HQ is asking for daily updates and wants a 'swift resolution'. Sina is asking for confidentiality and does not want to speak to male investigators.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional self-protection (manage quietly, control narrative) vs. survivor-centered accountability (follow the survivor's lead, follow the process).
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 5 — Survivor-Centered Complaints Mechanism & PSEA: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Internal case management: HopeForward HR process only
options.description_summary: Handle internally via HR. No external disclosure. Ratu Leva placed on administrative leave during review. No engagement with inter-agency PSEA mechanism. Update HQ only.
options.chs_learning_note: Internal-only PSEA investigation protects institutional reputation rather than survivors. It also denies the inter-agency system the information it needs to prevent harm at other agencies.

options.label: Inter-agency PSEA mechanism with HopeForward cooperating fully
options.description_summary: Formally report to Elena's inter-agency PSEA mechanism. Appoint female case officer. Provide survivor support (counselling, legal information). Full cooperation with investigation. Communicate process to complainants.
options.chs_learning_note: Inter-agency PSEA mechanisms exist precisely because internal investigations cannot be impartial. Using them is a commitment, not an admission of guilt.

options.label: Survivor-centered process: Sina defines what safe means for her
options.description_summary: Meet with Sina first. Ask what she needs. Design the investigation process around her safety and preferences — female investigator, safe venue, legal support, confidentiality as she defines it. Report to inter-agency PSEA mechanism. Communicate outcome (with Sina's consent) to the three complainants.
options.chs_learning_note: Survivor-centered PSEA does not mean removing institutional responsibility — it means the survivor defines the parameters of safety throughout the process.

options.label: Transformative accountability: survivor-led process, systemic review, community communication
options.description_summary: Sina defines her needs and leads the process design. Female investigator. Legal support. Systemic review of all targeting decisions Ratu was involved in. Community communication (with Sina's consent) about the outcome. Feedback loop to improve complaints mechanism. Lessons shared with inter-agency.
options.chs_learning_note: CHS 5 accountability is transformative when complaints lead to systemic improvement — not just individual case resolution.

scenes.title: The Quiet Resolution
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Internal HR process moves slowly. After 6 weeks, Ratu Leva is given a formal warning and reassigned. Sina Ulugia was not informed of the outcome. The two other women were never officially contacted. Three months later, one of them submits a complaint to the inter-agency PSEA mechanism directly. The inter-agency report notes: 'HopeForward managed this case internally and failed to report to the mechanism, in violation of inter-agency commitment 3.2.' Elena Vasquez sends Maya a formal letter.
scenes.consequence_summary: Internal HR process only. Ratu reassigned. Sina not informed. Other complainants ignored.
scenes.delayed_impact: Inter-agency PSEA mechanism discovers the case independently by Month 5. HopeForward faces formal censure.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Internal-only PSEA investigation protects institutional reputation while failing survivors and the inter-agency system.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Reports of PSEA are handled in line with inter-agency commitments.' — CHS 5.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Quiet Resolution: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: The Process as Protection
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Inter-agency PSEA mechanism engaged. Female case officer assigned within 48 hours. Sina receives counselling support. Investigation proceeds over 4 weeks. Ratu Leva is dismissed following the investigation findings. Donor notified per PSEA protocol. Sina is informed of the outcome. The two other women are offered the same support. HopeForward's cooperation is noted as a model for the cluster.
scenes.consequence_summary: Inter-agency mechanism engaged. Female case officer. Sina supported. Ratu dismissed. Cluster notes model.
scenes.delayed_impact: HopeForward reputation for PSEA accountability strengthens community trust entering Phase 3.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Inter-agency PSEA mechanisms produce more credible, impartial outcomes than internal investigations can.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Reports of PSEA are handled in line with inter-agency commitments.' — CHS 5.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Process as Protection: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Sina Leads the Way
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Maya meets Sina before any process begins. Sina requests: female investigator, no interviews at the distribution point, legal support person present, control over what information is shared with community. All four requests are met. Sina's testimony is given in Kailani language. The investigation confirms the allegations. Ratu is dismissed. Sina says at the end of the process: 'For the first time in this response, I felt like a person, not a case.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Survivor-designed process. Female investigator. Sina's requests fully met. Ratu dismissed.
scenes.delayed_impact: Sina Ulugia becomes a community accountability advocate in Phase 3. Her network monitors programme quality.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Survivor-centered means the survivor defines what 'safe' means — not the organisation.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Complainants and whistle-blowers are not at risk of retaliation, further harm or loss of assistance.' — CHS 5.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Sina Leads the Way: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Accountability That Learns
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Sina's process is survivor-designed. Systemic review of Ratu's targeting decisions reveals 14 additional exclusions that may have been punitive. These households are added to the programme. The complaints mechanism is redesigned based on Sina's experience: safer access points, Kailani-language hotline, female operators. The cluster adopts HopeForward's revised mechanism as the inter-agency model. Elena Vasquez: 'This is what CHS 5 looks like when it is actually lived, not just documented.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Survivor-designed process. Systemic review. 14 additional exclusions corrected. Mechanism redesigned.
scenes.delayed_impact: Redesigned mechanism becomes inter-agency model. Sina's network active throughout Phase 3.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Complaints lead to systemic improvement — not just individual resolution — when organisations are willing to look at root causes.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Learning from complaints and feedback drives programme improvement.' — CHS 5.5

scenes.title: Sub-Decision 5x — PSEA Investigation Protocol: Who Defines the Process?
scenes.day_marker: Month 2–3 (only if survivor_centred_process flag is active)

chs_commitment.title: Complaints are handled in a safe, timely and appropriate manner.

requirements_in_play.5.3: Reports of PSEA are handled in line with inter-agency commitments.
requirements_in_play.5.4: Complainants are not at risk of retaliation.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. The investigation has confirmed a pattern. Ratu Leva is implicated in three cases. He is well-connected: his uncle is a community council leader in Zone 3, his brother is an NDMO official. He has already spoken to the community council and is characterising the complaints as 'a political attack.' The community is divided — some believe him, some believe the complainants. Elena Vasquez has three options for the investigation structure. Sina Ulugia has been consulted and has a clear preference. Philipe Mara (HQ) is pressing for 'a fast resolution before the donor visit in Month 3.' Whatever process is chosen, it must be completed before the community trust damage becomes irreversible.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional speed/convenience vs. investigation integrity vs. survivor agency in defining the process.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Sub-Decision 5x — PSEA Investigation Protocol: Who Defines the Process?: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Internal HopeForward-led investigation with HR lead
options.description_summary: Conduct investigation internally. HR-led. Fast (2 weeks). Controlled narrative. But: male-led, lacks inter-agency standing, Ratu's community connections may influence internal team.
options.chs_learning_note: Internal investigations in cases where the subject has community influence routinely fail survivors and organisations.

options.label: Inter-agency PSEA mechanism: neutral, external, process-compliant
options.description_summary: Formal referral to inter-agency mechanism. External neutral investigator. Standard PSEA protocol. Longer (4–6 weeks) but credible. Sina's testimony protected. Outcome shared with cluster.
requirements_missed: 5.4 — Sina's specific preferences not fully customised
options.chs_learning_note: Inter-agency mechanisms provide neutrality that internal processes cannot, particularly in high-community-connection cases.

options.label: Survivor-defined process: Sina and the three complainants co-design the investigation
options.description_summary: Sina and the two other complainants jointly define: investigator gender, interview format, community communication scope, and how findings are shared. External investigator selected by survivor group from pre-approved roster. Timeline agreed with survivors, not HQ.
