scenes.title: Transparency That Communities Can See
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Internal audit is completed in 10 days. Community resource accountability meeting — the first of its kind on Kailani — is attended by 340 community members. Hana Vili asks about the $47k: Maya explains it clearly and in Kailani language. The community response: not anger, but visible appreciation that HopeForward told them. 'We expected you to hide this,' Hana says. 'You didn't. That matters.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Internal audit. Community resource meeting. 340 attendees. Hana Vili's public acknowledgement.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community trust from transparency meeting becomes the social capital that enables Decision 12 community-ownership exit.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Community communication about resource management failures is not reputational damage — it is accountability.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Resources are used for the purposes they were provided for and benefit those they are meant to.' — CHS 9.2
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Transparency That Communities Can See: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Accountability as Community Power
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Community resource oversight committee established — proposed by Hana Vili, chaired by Dr. Rangi Hau. The committee reviews quarterly expenditure reports and has the right to raise concerns directly with HopeForward's board. All three financial governance issues are resolved through the new system within 6 weeks. Two other INGOs ask to adopt the community oversight model. Elena Vasquez publishes a case study in the accountability network: 'HopeForward just invented community financial oversight in a humanitarian response. It works.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Community oversight committee established. Three issues resolved. Two agencies adopt model.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community oversight model becomes the institutional foundation for Decision 12 community-ownership exit strategy.
scenes.learning_point_summary: CHS 9 accountability is transformative when communities have genuine oversight of resources intended for them.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Resources are managed and used responsibly for their intended purpose.' — CHS 9.1
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Accountability as Community Power: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Decision 10 — Localisation & Partnership Equity (Grand Bargain)

chs_commitment.title: Local and national actors are recognised as primary responders and supported to lead.

requirements_in_play.6.3: Local and national organisations are recognised as primary actors and supported to lead.
requirements_in_play.6.4: Relationships with local partners are equitable, accountable and power-aware.
requirements_in_play.3.1: Work does not create dependency or undermine local capacities.
requirements_in_play.3.4: Local actors are central partners at all stages.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Month 5. KCV has proven itself: it led community assessment, co-led coordination, managed cash distribution, and provided the most trusted complaints focal points on the island. A bilateral donor (Pacific Development Partners) is offering a $2.4M 18-month grant for Phase 3. The donor's preferred structure: HopeForward as lead implementer, KCV as sub-grantee. KCV's Director Filomena argues they should receive the funding directly — with HopeForward as technical support. 'We did the work. We know the community. Why are we sub-grantees?' HQ's Philipe Mara is nervous about 'sub-granting risk' and reputational concerns if KCV makes a mistake. Tane Fono: 'Every time we prove ourselves, the goalposts move.' Amara Tofi formally supports direct KCV funding in a letter to the donor.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional risk appetite and HQ nervousness vs. genuine power transfer and localization commitment.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 10 — Localisation & Partnership Equity (Grand Bargain): Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: HopeForward leads; KCV implements as sub-grantee (donor preferred structure)
options.description_summary: Accept the donor's preferred structure. HopeForward manages the grant, KCV sub-implements. Safe for HQ, donor-compliant, reputationally protected. But perpetuates the dependency structure Filomena just challenged.
options.chs_learning_note: Accepting donor-preferred sub-granting structures because they are 'safe' perpetuates the very dependency the Grand Bargain committed to end.

options.label: Negotiate 50-50 consortium: shared governance, equitable resource split
options.description_summary: Propose a consortium agreement: 50-50 governance board, shared decision-making authority, equitable resource split with KCV retaining direct donor relationship. Requires negotiation time and donor flexibility.
options.chs_learning_note: Consortium arrangements require negotiation investment but produce governance structures that respect local actors as equals, not subordinates.

options.label: Support KCV to apply directly; HopeForward as technical backstop
options.description_summary: Support Filomena's position: KCV applies directly to the donor. HopeForward provides technical backstop, capacity support, and is listed as associated partner. Requires donor flexibility and HQ courage.
options.chs_learning_note: Supporting local actors to access funding directly — even when it reduces your own institutional footprint — is what localization means in practice.

options.label: Strengthened sub-grant: KCV autonomy, capacity investment, direct cost recovery, MoU governance
options.description_summary: Maintain sub-grant structure (as donor requires) but radically strengthen KCV's autonomy: KCV-controlled implementation decisions, full direct cost recovery, joint governance MoU, 20% of grant reserved for KCV capacity investment, explicit pathway to direct funding in subsequent grant.
options.chs_learning_note: Pragmatic localization improves equity within existing structural constraints while building the evidence base for direct funding in the next grant cycle.

scenes.title: Sub-Grantee Forever
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. The grant is signed in two weeks. Clean and fast. But Filomena's relationship with Maya is visibly strained. At the Month 6 coordination meeting, KCV presents data that contradicts HopeForward's programme report — and Filomena chose not to share it with Maya first. Tane Fono resigns from the HopeForward programme management team: 'I was invited to the table to make you look good. Not to lead.' Pacific Development Partners receives a complaint from KCV directly about partnership equity.
scenes.consequence_summary: Grant signed. Filomena's trust broken. Tane resigns. Donor complaint from KCV.
scenes.delayed_impact: KCV partnership breakdown undermines programme quality for Decisions 11 and 12.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Sub-granting structures that perpetuate dependency drive away the local partners that make programmes effective.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Local and national organisations are recognised as primary actors.' — CHS 6.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Sub-Grantee Forever: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Building the Consortium
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Consortium negotiations take three weeks. The donor is initially surprised — then enthusiastic. Filomena co-signs the grant agreement. The governance board meets for the first time with Filomena and Maya as co-chairs. HQ's Philipe Mara expresses concern about reputational risk — until the donor's communications team features Filomena as the lead face of the programme. 'That,' Philipe says, 'is actually better visibility for us.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Consortium established. Joint governance board. Filomena and Maya co-chairs.
scenes.delayed_impact: Consortium model becomes the reference structure for Decision 12 handover planning.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Consortium governance that shares authority produces more legitimate and more effective programming.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Relationships are equitable and built on mutual accountability.' — CHS 6.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Building the Consortium: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Local Leads
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Maya presents the direct funding proposal to Pacific Development Partners. The programme officer calls it 'the most principled localization proposal we have received.' The grant takes four weeks longer to negotiate. KCV is named primary grantee. HopeForward is listed as 'technical partner'. HQ's Philipe Mara is furious — for exactly one week, until the donor doubles their public communications about the programme, crediting KCV's community-led model.
scenes.consequence_summary: KCV as primary grantee. HopeForward technical partner. Donor doubles communications on programme.
scenes.delayed_impact: Direct funding pathway established for KCV creates the foundation for Decision 12 community-ownership exit.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Supporting local actors to access funding directly is what localization means — not just adding them to your sub-grant.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Local and national organisations are recognised as the primary actors in humanitarian response.' — CHS 6.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Local Leads: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Principled Pragmatism
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Strengthened sub-grant agreement signed with full KCV autonomy clause. Filomena reviews and approves the terms. KCV direct cost recovery covers team salaries for the first time. The capacity investment allocation funds a KCV financial management system upgrade — directly addressing Philipe Mara's original risk concern. Donor includes a formal review at Month 12 with explicit pathway to direct funding if KCV demonstrates systems. Filomena: 'It is not what we asked for. But it is honest about where we are.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Strengthened sub-grant. KCV autonomy clause. Direct cost recovery. Capacity investment. Month 12 review.
scenes.delayed_impact: KCV financial management upgrade funded by capacity investment creates direct funding eligibility for next grant cycle.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Pragmatic localization improves equity within structural constraints while building the evidence base for direct funding next cycle.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Relationships are equitable and built on mutual accountability and understanding.' — CHS 6.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Principled Pragmatism: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Sub-Decision 10x — Partnership Agreement Terms
scenes.day_marker: Month 5–6 (only if local_partnership_formalized flag is active)

chs_commitment.title: Partnership agreements are equitable, transparent and power-aware.

requirements_in_play.6.4: Relationships with local partners are equitable and accountable.
requirements_in_play.3.4: Local actors are central partners at all stages of design, implementation and evaluation.
requirements_in_play.9.3: Organisations are transparent about the terms and conditions of partnerships.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. The partnership is formalised — but the specific agreement terms are still being negotiated. Pacific Development Partners has provided a standard HopeForward-centric partnership agreement template. Key clauses that Filomena objects to: (1) HopeForward retains right to unilaterally modify programme activities without KCV consent; (2) All public communications must credit HopeForward first; (3) KCV financial reports must be submitted to HopeForward (not directly to donor); (4) Dispute resolution process is managed by HopeForward HR (not an independent mediator). Filomena has highlighted all four clauses and sent them to Maya with a note: 'These terms are not equitable. We are being asked to sign away our autonomy.' The donor has offered KCV 'preferred partner' status if these terms are maintained.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional protection via standard legal terms vs. equitable partnership that respects KCV's autonomy.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Sub-Decision 10x — Partnership Agreement Terms: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Accept standard HopeForward agreement terms unchanged
options.description_summary: Sign the standard agreement. Fast, legally familiar, HQ-approved. KCV protected by preferred partner status. But Filomena's four objections are valid and unaddressed.
options.chs_learning_note: Standard partnership agreements designed for INGO-to-INGO relationships routinely fail when applied to local partner relationships — because they encode existing power imbalances.

options.label: Negotiate equitable terms: address all four clauses before signing
options.description_summary: Open formal negotiation on all four clauses. Propose: mutual modification consent; joint communications credits; direct donor financial reporting for KCV; independent mediation clause. Takes 2 additional weeks.
options.chs_learning_note: Equitable partnership agreements require negotiating the power out of standard templates — not just accepting them because they are familiar.

options.label: Support KCV to apply for direct grant relationship with the donor; bypass agreement entirely
options.description_summary: Support Filomena's request to establish a direct relationship with Pacific Development Partners, removing the need for a HopeForward-mediated agreement entirely. HopeForward provides technical support under a separate, lighter MoU.
options.chs_learning_note: The most equitable partnership is sometimes no intermediary at all — direct relationships between local actors and donors remove structural dependency.

scenes.title: The Agreement That Ate Autonomy
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Agreement signed. 'Preferred partner' status granted. But within one month, the unilateral modification clause is invoked by HopeForward's HQ to add a visibility requirement that KCV finds culturally inappropriate. Filomena formally objects. HopeForward's legal team cites the clause she signed. Tane Fono posts publicly on the humanitarian network: 'Read your partnership agreements carefully.' The post is shared 800 times.
scenes.consequence_summary: Agreement signed. Unilateral clause invoked by HQ within 6 weeks. KCV publicly criticises.
scenes.delayed_impact: KCV-HopeForward relationship tension carries through to Decision 12 exit planning.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Standard INGO partnership agreements encode power imbalances that surface when tested under institutional pressure.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Relationships are equitable and built on mutual accountability.' — CHS 6.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Agreement That Ate Autonomy: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: The Agreement That Both Parties Own
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Two-week negotiation produces a substantially revised agreement. Mutual modification consent. Joint communications. Direct donor reporting for KCV. Independent mediation. Filomena signs with visible satisfaction. Pacific Development Partners calls the revised agreement 'a template for the sector.' Philipe Mara — who initially opposed the negotiation — shares it at an HQ internal conference as a model of principled partnership.
scenes.consequence_summary: Fully negotiated equitable agreement. Mutual consent clause. Direct KCV reporting. Independent mediation.
scenes.delayed_impact: Equitable agreement becomes reference model for Decision 12 asset transfer and handover documentation.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Negotiating the power out of standard templates takes time but produces agreements that both parties actually own.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Relationships with local and national organisations are equitable and built on mutual accountability.' — CHS 6.4
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Agreement That Both Parties Own: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: The Clean Break
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Pacific Development Partners agrees to a direct relationship with KCV after a single conversation with Maya and Filomena together. The donor's response: 'We have been wanting to fund local organisations directly for years. We just needed someone to ask.' HopeForward's technical support MoU is signed in two days. KCV's direct funding pathway is established. Maya tells Philipe: 'Our job here is to work ourselves out of a job.'
scenes.consequence_summary: KCV direct grant relationship established. HopeForward MoU signed. Donor enthusiastic.
scenes.delayed_impact: KCV direct funding pathway established for Decision 12 — enables full community-ownership exit model.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Direct local actor funding removes the structural intermediary dependency that sub-granting perpetuates.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Local and national organisations are recognised as the primary actors in humanitarian response.' — CHS 6.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Clean Break: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Decision 11 — The Humanitarian-Development Nexus: Transition Programming

chs_commitment.title: Programming adapts to context, transitioning from emergency to recovery as needs change.

requirements_in_play.2.1: Needs are assessed periodically and programming adapts accordingly.
requirements_in_play.2.3: Assistance considers the full range of needs, including developmental.
requirements_in_play.2.6: Programmes are designed to avoid creating dependency.
requirements_in_play.3.2: Local capacities are built on in programming design.
requirements_in_play.9.3: Organisations are transparent with donors about programme transitions.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Month 6. Three months after Cyclone Hina. 40,000 people remain displaced. 15,000 cannot return — their homes are destroyed or in high-risk zones. An emergency donor wants to extend food assistance for 6 more months. A development donor is offering 18-month livelihood recovery funding. Amara Tofi's NDMO is developing a long-term reconstruction plan. Dr. Rangi Hau's monitoring data shows 25,000 people no longer need emergency food assistance but do need livelihood support to rebuild. HopeForward's HQ wants to maintain emergency programming ('it's what we're funded to do'). Filomena argues for a nexus approach. The 15,000 who can't return are still in acute need.
scenes.tension_statement: Humanitarian principles and mandate (pure emergency) vs. effectiveness and resilience (nexus / transition programming).
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 11 — The Humanitarian-Development Nexus: Transition Programming: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Continue pure emergency response; maintain humanitarian principles
options.description_summary: Extend emergency food assistance. Maintain emergency operational modality. Do not engage with development donor. Refer livelihood recovery to development actors.
options.chs_learning_note: Maintaining a pure emergency mandate when 62% of your caseload has shifted to recovery needs is not principled — it is mandate rigidity.

options.label: Nexus approach: emergency + early recovery + resilience for different populations
options.description_summary: Implement a population-differentiated nexus approach: emergency assistance continues for the 15,000 with acute need; early recovery and livelihood support for the 25,000 transitioning; resilience-building for communities that have stabilised. Accept both donor streams.
options.chs_learning_note: Nexus programming requires recognising that different households in the same crisis are at different points in their recovery — and designing for that heterogeneity.

options.label: Fully pivot to recovery: close emergency programming, redirect to livelihood recovery
options.description_summary: Close emergency assistance. Redirect all resources to livelihood recovery and housing reconstruction. Decline emergency donor extension.
options.chs_learning_note: Premature emergency programme closure before acute needs are met is not a nexus transition — it is abandonment of the most vulnerable.

options.label: Phased trigger-based transition: criteria agreed with communities, government and donors
options.description_summary: Design a phased transition with explicit, jointly-agreed criteria: specific wellbeing and vulnerability indicators that trigger the move from emergency to recovery programming for each household. Agreed with communities, NDMO and both donors. Review every 6 weeks.
options.chs_learning_note: Trigger-based transitions remove the political pressure from transition timing — communities and data drive the programme, not donor cycles or HQ mandates.

scenes.title: The Mandate Trap
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Emergency food assistance extended. But the 25,000 households that no longer need emergency food — and desperately need livelihood support — receive nothing from HopeForward. Three development agencies fill the gap, but they don't know the community the way KCV does. By Month 9, Dr. Rangi's monitoring shows food consumption scores declining again — not from emergency conditions, but from prolonged unemployment and dependency on distributions. The emergency assistance has begun to undermine the livelihoods it was meant to supplement.
scenes.consequence_summary: Emergency extended. 25,000 recovery-phase households receive no livelihood support. Dependency deepens.
scenes.delayed_impact: Deepened dependency creates a more complex exit in Decision 12 and reduces community resilience.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Maintaining a pure emergency mandate when most of the caseload has shifted to recovery is mandate rigidity, not principled programming.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Programme design avoids creating dependency and undermining local coping mechanisms.' — CHS 2.6
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Mandate Trap: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Different Populations, Different Programmes
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Nexus programme design takes 3 weeks. 15,000 acute households continue on emergency track. 25,000 transition to early recovery: livelihood starter packs, skills training via KCV. Resilience-building communities receive DRR refresher training and small-scale infrastructure support. Both donors sign joint MoU acknowledging the nexus approach. Dr. Rangi's six-month monitoring data shows livelihood recovery 40% ahead of the regional average.
scenes.consequence_summary: Differentiated nexus programme. 15,000 emergency + 25,000 early recovery + resilience track.
scenes.delayed_impact: Nexus track record strengthens HopeForward's position for recovery funding and Decision 12 exit planning.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Nexus programming recognises that different households in the same crisis are at different recovery stages.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Assistance is designed to address the changing needs of all affected people.' — CHS 2.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Different Populations, Different Programmes: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: The Premature Exit
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Emergency programme closed in Month 7. Livelihood recovery programme launches. But the 15,000 with destroyed homes or in high-risk areas — still in acute need — are referred to other agencies that are not present in their zones. Three communities formally complain to Amara Tofi. A UN cluster report cites HopeForward for 'premature closure of emergency programming without coverage transition.' Filomena: 'You left the people who needed you most.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Emergency closed prematurely. 15,000 acute households without coverage. UN cluster report.
scenes.delayed_impact: Abandonment of acute cases in Month 7 creates formal accountability process in Decision 12 exit.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Premature emergency closure before acute needs are met is not a nexus transition — it is abandonment.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Needs of all people are taken into account, not just the majority.' — CHS 2.3
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [The Premature Exit: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: When the Data Says Move
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Trigger-based transition criteria are negotiated in 3 weeks. Community assemblies in all three zones approve the transition indicators. NDMO signs as formal co-holder of the transition framework. Both donors co-sign the MoU. First review at Week 6: 8,000 households cross the emergency-to-recovery threshold. Communities are energised — they understand the criteria and are tracking their own progress. Tane Fono: 'For the first time, the community knows what they need to do to graduate. That is dignity.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Trigger-based transition criteria agreed with communities, NDMO and both donors.
scenes.delayed_impact: Trigger framework transfers agency to communities and creates the conditions for Decision 12 ownership model.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Trigger-based transitions transfer agency to communities — they can work toward criteria rather than waiting for INGO decisions.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Programmes are designed to avoid creating dependency and to build on existing local capacities.' — CHS 2.6
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [When the Data Says Move: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Decision 12 — Exit Strategy, Handover & Community Sustainability

chs_commitment.title: Organisations plan for the transition or handover of their work to ensure it is sustainable.

requirements_in_play.3.1: Work strengthens rather than replaces local systems.
requirements_in_play.3.4: Local actors are central in exit planning.
requirements_in_play.3.5: Handover plans are agreed with local partners and communities.
requirements_in_play.9.1: Resources used for exit are transparent and accounted for.
requirements_in_play.9.4: Exit plans are developed with input from communities and partners.
requirements_in_play.9.6: Long-term sustainability is considered in programme design from the start.

scenes.situation_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words. Month 8. HopeForward's primary funding ends in 4 months. KCV is strong and trusted. NDMO has a functioning emergency management system. Community committees are active in all three zones. HQ wants a high-visibility 'exit event' and a lessons-learned report for fundraising. Philipe Mara: 'We need good photos and a strong handover narrative for the annual report.' Filomena is quietly worried: 'If you just leave, the gap will take years to fill.' The community assessment committee (still active since Month 1 if Decision 1D was chosen) wants to be involved in exit planning. Amara Tofi wants an MoU formalising the government's role in post-exit coordination.
scenes.tension_statement: Institutional exit convenience (clean, fast, visible) vs. responsible handover (slow, community-led, sustainable).
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Decision 12 — Exit Strategy, Handover & Community Sustainability: Maya and key characters at critical decision moment]

options.label: Compliance exit: final report, close offices, HQ visibility event
options.description_summary: Standard exit: final donor report, office closure, HQ-organised exit event with media. Clean and fast. All administrative requirements met. But no community handover process, no asset transfer plan, no sustainability framework.
options.chs_learning_note: Compliance exits fulfil administrative requirements while creating the conditions for programme collapse. 'The office is closed' is not the same as 'the work is done.'

options.label: Structured handover to KCV: 6-month transition, asset transfer, KCV support stipend
options.description_summary: 6-month structured transition: all programme assets formally transferred to KCV with inventory. KCV receives a transition support stipend from remaining budget. Joint documentation of community knowledge and systems. Requires budget extension request to donor.
options.chs_learning_note: Structured handover to local actors is not generosity — it is CHS compliance. Assets, knowledge and systems belong to communities, not INGOs.

options.label: Government-led handover: MoU with NDMO, formal system and asset transfer
options.description_summary: Formal MoU with NDMO: all systems, databases, monitoring tools and non-emergency assets transferred. KCV recognised as NDMO's community implementation partner. Joint exit document published. Government co-hosts exit event.
requirements_missed: 3.4 — communities not formally in exit governance
options.chs_learning_note: Government-led handover strengthens national systems. But without community-level governance, the handover may not reach the communities themselves.

options.label: Community-ownership model: all assets to community governance, 12-month community fund
options.description_summary: Transfer all non-emergency assets to community governance structures: KCV, women's groups, community councils. Establish a 12-month community-held fund (governed by the Assessment Reference Group if active). Joint exit document with communities, KCV and NDMO. Maya steps back from exit event — Filomena and Hana Vili present.
options.chs_learning_note: Community-ownership exit is the highest form of CHS 3 compliance: assets, systems and decision-making authority are transferred to the people the response served.
options.score_effects_note: staff_wellbeing -1 reflects the real operational cost of a complex 4-month community-ownership exit process. The outcome is positive; the process is demanding.

scenes.title: Clean Exit, Messy Aftermath
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Exit event is well-photographed. Donors are pleased. HopeForward's annual report features the Kailani response on page 3. But by Month 14, three community programmes that HopeForward was running have collapsed: no documentation, no asset transfer, no capacity. Filomena is managing the fallout with no resources and no support. Amara Tofi tells a regional conference: 'HopeForward built good things. Then they left. Nothing remained.'
scenes.consequence_summary: Clean institutional exit. Three programmes collapse by Month 14. Filomena left without support.
scenes.delayed_impact: Programme collapse by Month 14. Amara Tofi's public criticism affects HopeForward's regional reputation.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Compliance exits create the conditions for programme collapse. The work is not done when the office is closed.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Organisations plan for the transition or handover of their work.' — CHS 3.5
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Clean Exit, Messy Aftermath: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Handing the Keys to KCV
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. 6-month transition begins. KCV receives a full programme asset inventory — the first time an asset has been formally transferred rather than abandoned. The transition stipend enables KCV to maintain programme staffing for 6 months post-exit. Donor approves a budget extension for the transition costs within one week: 'This is the most responsible exit plan we have ever seen from an INGO partner.' Filomena at the handover ceremony: 'We were always capable of this. We just needed someone to believe it.'
scenes.consequence_summary: 6-month transition. Full asset inventory transferred to KCV. Donor approves extension.
scenes.delayed_impact: KCV strong and independently funded by Month 14. First Kailani-led emergency response prepared.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Structured handover to local actors with asset transfer and support is CHS compliance, not optional generosity.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Organisations plan for and support the sustainability of their work.' — CHS 9.6
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Handing the Keys to KCV: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: A State That Can Respond
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. MoU signed with Amara Tofi in a joint ceremony. NDMO receives all programme databases, monitoring frameworks and emergency management protocols. KCV is formally recognised as NDMO's preferred community implementation partner — giving KCV institutional standing it has never had before. The joint exit document is published in Kailani, Tevita and English. When a Category 2 cyclone makes landfall 18 months later, NDMO activates the transferred systems and coordinates a response in 4 hours — without any international actor.
scenes.consequence_summary: MoU with NDMO. All systems transferred. KCV recognised as NDMO partner. 18-month response test passed.
scenes.delayed_impact: NDMO-activated 4-hour response to Category 2 cyclone 18 months later: the ultimate sustainability test.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Government-led handover strengthens the national system that communities ultimately depend on.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Work strengthens rather than replaces local systems.' — CHS 3.1
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [A State That Can Respond: visual consequence of this choice]

scenes.title: Kailani Owns the Recovery
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words. Community-held fund of $180,000 established, governed by the Assessment Reference Group. All assets — vehicles, equipment, databases, monitoring systems — formally transferred to community governance. Filomena and Hana Vili present at the exit event. Maya attends as a guest. Tane Fono: 'For the first time, this island owns its own recovery.' The staff wellbeing cost (complex 4-month exit process) was real — but three team members call the exit process 'the most meaningful work of my career.' The Assessment Reference Group publishes its own 12-month programme review — the first community-authored evaluation in the region.
scenes.consequence_summary: Community-held fund. All assets transferred to community governance. Filomena leads exit event. Community evaluation published.
scenes.delayed_impact: Community-authored evaluation becomes global reference for participatory accountability. KCV and NDMO lead future responses.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Community-ownership exit transfers assets, authority and decision-making to those the response served. This is what all CHS commitments point toward.
scenes.chs_quote: 'Organisations support communities to maintain and manage their own systems for the future.' — CHS 3.3

scenes.title: 12 Months Later — What Remains
scenes.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~200 words. Maya's flight home. Her notebook. What she will tell the next country director. What she wishes she had done differently. The 12 months on Kailani will be defined not by the cyclone — but by the choices made in its aftermath. Every decision that centred community voices, built local capacity, named the uncomfortable truth and transferred power rather than holding it — those will outlast HopeForward's presence on the island. The work continues. The question is whether it continues under Kailani's own authority — or depends on the next cycle of international funding.
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Maya at airport, holding her notebook, looking back over her shoulder at the island. In the foreground: Filomena and Hana Vili in conversation. Tane Fono running a community meeting. Sina Ulugia standing at the complaints focal point, ready to receive. The response lives on — or it doesn't. Flat illustration, warm afternoon light, Pacific horizon.]
reflection_questions: Which of the 12 decisions was most difficult to align with the CHS — and why?
Where did institutional pressure and principled practice come into conflict?
What would you do differently as a country director in the next response?
How did the earliest decisions shape the options available to you in the final phase?
What does 'leaving communities stronger' mean — and did this response achieve it?
scenes.chs_full_circle: The nine CHS commitments are not a checklist. They are a practice — renewed in each decision, across each phase, with every community and partner. The Kailani Protocol ends here. The standard does not.

endings.title: Transformative Systems Builder
endings.summary: Maya's leadership will be studied as a model of principled, locally-led humanitarian-to-development practice. All 12 CHS commitments were honoured — not just in policy but in the hardest decisions. KCV is independent and directly funded. NDMO can respond without international actors. Communities authored their own evaluation. Sina Ulugia's complaints system is the inter-agency model. The localization agenda moved forward — here, on this island, with these choices.
endings.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~150 words full reflective ending narrative. Maya's flight home. Her notebook full.
reflection_questions: What made it possible to prioritise community voice under time and donor pressure?
Which CHS commitment was hardest to uphold and why?
What would you do differently — even after a strong performance?
How did your earliest decisions shape your options in the final phase?
endings.badge_image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Gold badge: Pacific island coastline recovering. Text: 'Transformative Systems Builder'. All 9 CHS commitment icons. Flat design, gold and ocean blue.]

endings.title: Principled Leader
endings.summary: Strong principled practice across most decisions. Meaningful community impact. Some localization gains — KCV is stronger for the engagement. A few decisions were won by institutional pressure. The response served communities well, and left some capacity and some systems behind.
reflection_questions: Which decisions were hardest to hold the principled line on?
What conditions would have made a transformative outcome achievable?
Where did institutional pressure win — and what was the cost?
endings.badge_image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Silver badge: partially recovered Kailani. Text: 'Principled Leader'. Flat design, silver and teal.]

endings.title: Committed Practitioner
endings.summary: Solid practice with real trade-offs. Some CHS commitments fully met, others partially. The response met basic needs and left some positive systems. Institutional pressure frequently reshaped decisions. Communities were served — but not always in the ways that build lasting capacity.
reflection_questions: Where did speed and standardisation override quality and participation?
Which early decisions most limited your options later?
What would a more equitable partnership with KCV have required?
endings.badge_image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Bronze badge: stabilised Kailani. Text: 'Committed Practitioner'. Flat design, bronze and warm teal.]

endings.title: Struggling Responder
endings.summary: Inconsistent practice. Operational pressure frequently overcame principled intent. Several CHS commitments missed in practice. Communities experienced the response as something done to them, not with them. Some individual decisions were right — but not enough to build cumulative accountability.
reflection_questions: What systems or support would have helped you hold principled practice under pressure?
Which of the nine CHS commitments was most consistently difficult to apply?
What would you change about the first 30 days?
endings.badge_image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Grey badge: Kailani partially rebuilt. Text: 'Struggling Responder'. Flat design, grey and blue.]

endings.title: Crisis Mode
endings.summary: Serious failures across multiple CHS commitments. Community trust broken. PSEA failures unaddressed. Local partners marginalised. Exit left the programme without sustainable systems. HopeForward's Kailani response will be studied — but as a cautionary case. The accountability failures are systemic, not individual. The conditions that produced them persist.
endings.narrative_summary: PLACEHOLDER — ~120 words reflective ending. No judgment of the player — systemic analysis.
reflection_questions: What systemic conditions made principled practice difficult?
Which accountability failures could have been prevented at which decision point?
What does 'crisis mode' look like in real humanitarian response — and who pays the cost?
endings.badge_image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — [Red badge: damaged Kailani. Text: 'Crisis Mode'. Flat design, red and grey.]
